coredata.dc:title: In situ assembly of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots surrounded iron(III) oxide nanocomposite; a novel electrocatalyst for highly sensitive detection of antipsychotic drug olanzapine
coredata.dc:creator: Muthusankar, Ganesan
Sangili, Arumugam
Chen, Shen-Ming
Karkuzhali, Rajendran
Sethupathi, Murugan
Gopu, Gopalakrishnan
Karthick, Selvam
Devi, Ramdhass Keerthika
Sengottuvelan, Nallathambi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report the synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) chemically incorporated with iron oxide nanoparticles (S-CQDs/Fe2O3) using hydrothermal synthesis strategy. The as-synthesized nanocomposite was characterized using powder X-ray diffraction, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscope, thermogravimetric analysis, and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. We have analyzed the clear electrochemical kinetics of the S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode. Addition to this, electrochemical sensing property of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE towards the detection of antipsychotic drug olanzapine (OLZ) was explored. Remarkably, S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE has shown the good electrocatalytic activity for OLZ oxidation and also exhibits a very low detection limit and sensitivity of about 0.006 μM and 0.88 μA μM−1 cm−2 respectively. Further, the proposed sensor has applied for the detection of OLZ in urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Sulfur doping
Fe2O3
Olanzapine
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the UGC – BSR ( 25-1/2014–2015(BSR)/7–88/2007 (BSR), Dt.:15th-Oct-2015) fellowship, India and the Alagappa University Research Fund (AURF) (AU: AURF Start-up Research Grant: 2015-16, Dt.: 10.02.2016) and The Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST 106-2113-M-027-003 ) for the financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) PXRD pattern, (B) FT-IR and (C) TGA curve for S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite.
(A–B) TEM images of S-CQDs at different magnification.
(A–C) TEM images, (D) EDS and (E–H) elemental mapping of S-CQD/Fe2O3 nanocomposite.

(A) CVs of different modified electrode at scanning rate of 50 mV s−1, (B) different scanning rates of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE (inset: linear plot of anodic and cathodic peak current versus the square root of the scanning rate) in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl solution.

(A) CV response of different composites, (B) anodic peak current intensity difference of the different composite, (C) CV response of S-CQD/Fe2O3/GCE with different films and (D) anodic peak current intensity difference of the different films in 200 μM OLZ at 0.1 M PB solution (pH 7).
CVs of S-CQD/Fe2O3/GCE (A) different concentration of OLZ (a = absence; b–f = 50–250 μM OLZ) and (B) plot of anodic peak current versus the concentration of OLZ.
(A) Different scanning rates of 200 μM OLZ from 20 to 180 mV s−1 and (B) plot of anodic peak current versus the square root of the scanning rate.
DPVs of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE (A) increasing OLZ concentration of (a to l = 0.1–35 μM) and (B) calibration curve of the peak current on the OLZ concentrations. Scanning rate: 50 mV S−1.
Variation in DPV current response of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE for OLZ in the presence of additions of different biological compounds and ions.
CVs found at (A) accumulation time, (B) reproducibility, (C) 50 continuous cycles of the S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE without and (D) with 200 μM of OLZ at a scanning rate: 50 mV s−1.

DPV curves of the S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE in (A) urine sample 1, (B) urine sample 2 containing OLZ with PB solution (pH 7) and (C, D) calibration plot for the OLZ spiked urine sample 1 and urine sample 2 respectively. Scanning rate: 50 mV s−1.
Schematic illustration of the hydrothermal synthesis of S-CQD/Fe2O3 nanocomposite and its electrocatalytic effect towards OLZ.
Analytical parameters for OLZ determination by various methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: j
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Gold nanoparticles/glutamine modified carbon paste electrode.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DPV results obtained from S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE for the detection of OLZ in human urine samples.

head.ce:title: In situ assembly of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots surrounded iron(III) oxide nanocomposite; a novel electrocatalyst for highly sensitive detection of antipsychotic drug olanzapine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi 630003, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, National Taipei University of Technology, No. 1, Section 3, Chung-Hsiao East Road, Taipei 106, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report the synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) chemically incorporated with iron oxide nanoparticles (S-CQDs/Fe2O3) using hydrothermal synthesis strategy. The as-synthesized nanocomposite was characterized using powder X-ray diffraction, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscope, thermogravimetric analysis, and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. We have analyzed the clear electrochemical kinetics of the S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode. Addition to this, electrochemical sensing property of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE towards the detection of antipsychotic drug olanzapine (OLZ) was explored. Remarkably, S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE has shown the good electrocatalytic activity for OLZ oxidation and also exhibits a very low detection limit and sensitivity of about 0.006 μM and 0.88 μA μM−1 cm−2 respectively. Further, the proposed sensor has applied for the detection of OLZ in urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Olanzapine (OLZ, 2‑methyl‑4‑(4‑methyl‑1‑piperazinyl)‑10H‑thieno(2,3‑b)(1,5) benzodiazepine) is a widely used U.S. Food and drug administration approved antipsychotic drug for the medication of schizophrenia and related illnesses such as alogia, avolition, and anhedonia. It is also effective against the positive symptoms (delusions and hallucinations) and negative symptoms (social withdrawal and poverty of speech) of schizophrenia. On comparison with other antipsychotics, OLZ possesses the peculiar property of antagonistic activities on dopaminergic, serotonin and/or adrenergic receptors [1,2]. After oral dosing OLZ is well absorbed and widely metabolized into more primary metabolites (N-oxide, 4′‑N‑glucuronide, N‑desmethyl, 10‑N‑glucuronide and 2‑hydroxymethyl metabolites) [3]. Owing to its potential use, it is needed to analyze OLZ in biological fluids with quick, effective and economical methods. To achieve this purpose, electrochemical techniques are effective than other analytical techniques. Even though OLZ is an electro-active molecule, it shows less electrochemical response and electrode sediment problem at bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) [1–4]. Thus, it needs an advance electrode material for the detection of OLZ in pharmaceutical forms and biological fluids.

Recently, different kinds of nanomaterials have been used to modify bare electrode for OLZ detection. Behzad et al. [1] fabricated an OLZ sensor based on organic derivative with quantum dots modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes composite. Arvand et al. [4] reported an amine-functionalized TiO2 modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes on glassy carbon electrode for the determination of OLZ. The same research group investigated the OLZ determination based on core/shell magnetic nanocomposite [5]. Mashhadizadeh et al. [6] investigated the electrochemistry of OLZ on ZnS nanoparticles modified carbon paste electrode. Ahmed et al. [7] employed gold nanoparticles for the sensitive determination of OLZ in tablets and urine samples. However, there is a need for the development of novel nanoparticles-based electrode materials that can determine OLZ in pure and in biological fluids with higher sensitivity. In this regard, Fe2O3 receives more consideration due to the most stable structure of iron oxide under the room temperature, an intrinsic enzyme-like activity, low toxic, low cost and good in biological tolerance [8]. However, the conductivity of single component Fe2O3 is inadequate, that reduces its extensive application in electrochemical sensing applications. To overcome the poor conductivity in metal oxide nanostructures, carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene [9,10] reduced graphene oxide [11], carbon nanotubes [12] and graphitic carbon nitride [13] have been employed. Therefore, it is expected that a new class of carbon-based material; carbon quantum dot decoration over the surface of Fe2O3 nanoparticles (Fe2O3 NPs) may improve the conductivity and electrochemical sensitivity.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the new type of carbon-based nanomaterial extremely small in size and also a 0D material. The CQDs comprise amorphous to the nanocrystalline core with an external shell made up of various functional groups [14]. Recently CQDs have widely been used as a fluorescence sensor [15–17], and biosensor [18,19] due to their excellent optical and biocompatible properties. Recent studies have also demonstrated that CQDs can enhance the electrochemical reactivity of important biomolecules as well simplify determination of pharmacological substances [20,21] because of their unique electrical properties. Heteroatoms (boron (B), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S) and phosphorous (P)) doping in the carbon nanomaterials highly alter the band gap and extend their application in many fields [21–23]. Tuning the energy gap in carbon-based materials with dopants offers probable applications in electro devices. Moreover, this being a hopeful approach which can modulate their electron density and improve the chemical activity, which was testified through theoretically and experimentally [24,25]. There are very few reports seen in the literature, for the synthesis of CQDs decorated Fe2O3 composites. Recently Pramanik et al. have reported the magnetic CQDs for the application of superbugs’ separation [26]. Kumar et al. have synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot with γ-Fe2O3 fluorescent nanocomposite for neuron imaging [27]. Zhang et al. have prepared Fe2O3/carbon quantum dot nanocubes for photocatalytic application [28]. Still, electrochemical sensors based on CQDs with Fe2O3 NPs are very rare.

Considering the above-mentioned properties of Fe2O3 and heteroatom doped CQDs, we have imported together these nanomaterials for the effective electro-catalytic performance with unique synergetic effects. The S-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) were synthesized using simple hydrothermal method from sulfur-containing organic molecule. Then S-CQDs were surrounded on the surface Fe2O3 NPs by in-situ solvothermal method to improve the electrical conductivity. Hereafter, S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite was fabricated for the OLZ sensing. The proposed sensor possesses simple electrode preparation, good linear range, operational stability, sensitivity, selectivity and the very low detection limit for the determination of OLZ in real samples. A thorough search of the relevant literature did not yield any research work on applying the proposed electrode for sensing of OLZ.

p‑Toluenesulfonic acid (CH3C6H4SO3H·H2O, ≥98.5%) (p‑TSA), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 97.0%), polyethylene glycol (PEG 4000), sodium acetate (C2H3NaO2, ≥99.0%) and olanzapine (C17H20N4S, ≥98.0%) (OLZ) were analytical grade chemicals, purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India and used as such in the received condition without further purification. The stock solution of OLZ (10 mM) was prepared by ethanol and stored at 4 °C. Different pH values of phosphate buffer (PB) solutions were prepared by standard solutions of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 and pH values were adjusted with NaOH. The solutions were prepared using 18 MΩ De-ionized water.

The crystal structure of as-synthesized nanocomposite was studied using powder X-ray diffractometer (PXRD) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.541 Å). The Debye–Scherrer formula, D = Kλ/βCOS θ, was used to calculate the average diameter of the crystals. Where D is crystallite size, λ is wavelength of the radiation, K (=0.89) is constant value linked to shape of the crystal, β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) in radians and θ is the diffraction angle. The functional groups were identified from the FT-IR spectra, recorded in FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker IFS28 spectrometer). Thermal stability was calculated from the thermogravimetric (TGA) analyzer (Netzsch TG-2019 series) at 10 °C min−1 heating rate under inert gas condition. The structural morphology and elemental studies were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) (JEOL 2000) respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were studied with three electrode systems, using glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as working electrode, saturated Ag/AgCl as reference electrode and platinum wire (Pt) as counter electrode. All the electrochemical studies were carried out in an inert gas (N2) condition on CHI 400 and 900 work station.

S-CQDs were synthesized from p-TSA, via hydrothermal method with a slight modification [21]. Initially, 2 g of p-TSA was dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol and stirred to get the homogeneous solution. Then the colorless solution was transferred into 50 mL autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After this, autoclave was allowed to cool naturally and the obtained pale yellow colored solution was diluted with 75 mL of water and neutralized with 0.1 M NaOH. A syringe filter with pore size 0.22 μm, was used to filter the large particles in the solution. After the filtration, transparent pale-yellow color S-CQDs was obtained.

The S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite was synthesized through the reported method with certain modification [8]. FeCl3·6H2O (0.40 g) was taken in ethylene glycol (16 mL) and mechanically stirred to get a clear solution. Then sodium acetate (1.44 g) and PEG 4000 (0.4 g) were added to the above-mentioned solution and stirred well to obtain homogeneous solution. Then 15 mL of S-CQDs was added drop by drop to the solution. Further, the mixture solution is taken in an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h and allowed to cool in room temperature. The obtained reddish-brown color precipitate was washed thrice with water and ethanol, later dried at 80 °C to get the pure S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite. The synthesis procedure is shown in Scheme 1 .

5 mg of S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite was well dispersed in 1 mL of water under ultra-sonic treatment for 30 min to get the red color suspension. At the same time, GCE surface was polished using alumina powder to get a mirror-like surface, then rinsed with DI water and dried. An optimized concentration of about 8 μL of the suspension was drop cast on the cleaned GCE and then dried in an air oven at 50 °C for 30 min.

The PXRD pattern of the as-synthesized S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite is displayed in Fig. 1A, which confirms that Fe2O3 in S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite is α-Fe2O3 phase. The diffraction peaks at 24.4°, 33.2°, 35.7°, 41.1°, 43.2°, 49.74°, 54.1°, 57.3°, 62.7°, 64.3° and 72° correspond to those α-Fe2O3 crystal plans (012), (104), (110), (113), (202), (024), (116), (122), (214), (300) and (1010) respectively. The obtained diffracted patterns are well matched with rhombohedral structure α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS #33-0664) [29]. Notably, a broadband (20°–30°) centered at 26° in S-CQD/Fe2O3 nanocomposite, is the characteristic pattern for S-CQD. The PXRD pattern confirms the formation of S-CQDs/Fe2O3 composite and the absence of impurity peaks also confirms the purity of the material. From the Debye-Scherrer's formula, the average crystallite size is calculated of about 40 nm.

The functional groups of S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite are analyzed by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1B). Two sharp peaks at 456 and 557 cm−1 are the characteristic FeO bond vibration of α-Fe2O3 in the nanocomposite. In addition to that, absorption bands of OH deformation (1063 cm−1), epoxy symmetrical deformation (1209 cm−1), carbon-carbon (CC) double bond stretching (1406 cm−1), carbonyl (CO) stretching (1621 cm−1) and hydroxyl group (OH) vibration (3364 cm−1) are also observed, suggesting that S-CQDs are indeed present in the nanocomposite. Thus, FT-IR spectrum also confirms that the product is S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite. Further, the thermal stability of nanocomposite is studied using TGA and the result is shown in Fig. 1C. A weight loss to below 100 °C is due to the loss of adsorbed water. The regular weight loss up to 800 °C can be attributed to the elimination of more stable oxygen functional groups and volatile compounds such as CH4, CO, H2, and CO2 from S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite [30].

The surface morphology of the S-CQDs and nanocomposite was investigated by TEM. TEM images of S-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 , which displays the even dispersion of the S-CQDs with a diameter of about 5 nm. Fig. 3A–C shows the TEM images of S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite in different magnifications, revealing that the nanoparticles are uniform and monodispersed. The calculated average crystallite size from the XRD pattern is well matched with TEM results. Fig. 3C shows the distinct contrast between the α-Fe2O3 and S-CQDs, and also confirms the formation of S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite. EDS and corresponding elemental mappings are displayed in Fig. 3D–H respectively. The composition of the nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 3D and the elemental mapping confirms the existence of elements such as C, O, Fe and S and its uniform dispersion in the nanocomposite.

Electrochemical detecting features of the bare GCE, S-CQDs/GCE, Fe2O3/GCE and S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE were analyzed by CV in a solution of 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 system, at a scanning rate of 50 mV s−1. Fig. 4A shows a pair of redox peaks at the surface of GCE, S-CQDs/GCE, Fe2O3/GCE and S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE with the cathodic and anodic peak potential difference (∆E p) of 108, 95, 91 and 85 mV respectively. These results suggest that the fast electrocatalytic redox reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 occurs at the S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE surface with higher redox peak current and lower ∆E p when compared with other modified electrodes. Fig. 4B exhibits different scanning rate on the surface of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. When increasing the scanning rate from 10 to 200 mV s−1 anodic and cathodic peak currents are increased. A plot between the redox peaks current (I pa) and the square root of the scanning rate gives a linear plot (inset, Fig. 4B). The Randles–Sevcik Eq. (1) is used to calculate the effective surface area of various modified electrode [31].
(1)
I p
=
2.69 ×
10 5
n
3 / 2
D
1 / 2
ν
1 / 2
A
C ∗
where I p denotes the anodic peak current, n is the total number of electron transferred (n = 1), D is the diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 (7.6 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), ν is the scanning rate, A is the effective surface area and C* is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4. From the Eq. (1), the effective surface areas of GCE, S-CQDs/GCE, Fe2O3/GCE and S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE are calculated as 0.066, 0.089, 0.091 and 0.098 cm2 respectively. Hence, the experimental data suggest that the S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite exhibit fast electron transfer and huge electroactive surface area on comparing to the S-CQDs, Fe2O3, and bare GCE. Therefore, S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE is employed for the further electrochemical investigation.

To evaluate the electrochemical behavior of OLZ, CV was recorded at bare GCE, S-CQDs/GCE, Fe2O3/GCE and S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE in the potential range of 0.0 to 0.6 V with 200 μM OLZ in 0.1 M PB solution (pH 7) at the scanning rate of 50 mV s−1 as shown in Fig. 5A. From CV curves it is observed that the bare GCE shows mild oxidation peak at 0.35 V with small peak current. Electrochemical kinetics was improved by the use of S-CQDs/GCE electrode where anodic peak current observed at 0.29 V with higher peak current than bare GCE. This can be accredited to OLZ oxidation due to the hydrogen bond formation between the diazepine ring of OLZ and the carbonyl functional group [7] of the S-CQDs. So, the bond energy between oxygen and hydrogen is weakened and the electron transfer was possibly occurred via N⋯HO bond. Thus, S-CQDs can act as a promoter to raise the rate of electron transfer for OLZ oxidation. Whereas, negatively shifted sharp oxidation at 0.23 V with significant increase in peak current results from Fe2O3/GCE due to its higher surface area and better catalytic behavior. Further, in comparison with S-CQDs/GCE, Fe2O3/GCE shows higher oxidation current with lower oxidation potential for OLZ. This result suggesting that effective electrocatalytic oxidation is highly favorable when the electrode is modified with Fe2O3 NPs than S-CQDs. Interestingly, S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE shows oxidation at 0.23 V with enhanced peak current than the Fe2O3/GCE, S-CQDs/GCE, and bare GCE. The enhancement in anodic peak current can be attributed to the higher catalytic effect of Fe2O3 NPs and the existence of conductive S-CQDs over the surface of Fe2O3 NPs. Furthermore, hydrogen bond between diazepine ring of OLZ and carbonyl group of S-CQDs facilitate the specific interaction with target molecule. Further, S-CQDs/Fe2O3 can assist the direct electron transfer between the OLZ and the bulk electrode surface. Thus, the observed improvement of the peak current response in S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE is in agreement with proposal that the presence of S-CQD enhances the conductivity of α-Fe2O3 NPs. Therefore, S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite is directed to easing in electron transfer process of OLZ and improved the conductivity of the electrode and offers good electrocatalytic performance towards the electro-oxidation of OLZ.

The effect of pH plays an important role in the electro-oxidation of OLZ because it shows the influence in peak potential and current. Fig. 5B shows the peak current intensities and potentials at different pH condition. When the pH is increased from 5 to 7 the oxidation peak current increases, beyond pH 7 anodic peak current tend to decrease. While, anodic peak potential value linearly decreased with pH and can be represented by Epa (V) = −0.0612(±0.002) pH + 0.6679(±0.019) with R2 = 0.9919 (n = 5). Hence, the slope value 0.0612 V is related to the expected 0.059 V for two electrons and two protons transfer process [5]. From Fig. 5B, it is observed that at higher the pH lower the potential is resulted for the OLZ oxidation. Further, the maximum anodic peak current is attained at pH 7. Therefore, pH 7 is preferred as an optimal condition for the further electrochemical study.

The CV response of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE in 0.1 M PB solution (pH 7) containing various concentrations of OLZ at a scanning rate of 50 mV s−1 is shown in Fig. 6A. Without concentration of OLZ (a), S-CQD/Fe2O3/GCE does not show any notable peak; at the same time, a separate oxidation peak is observed in the presence of 50 μM OLZ at 0.23 V. Besides, anodic peak current of OLZ increases with increasing the concentration from 50 to 250 μM OLZ on the surface of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE. The obtained results specify the good electro-oxidation behavior of OLZ at S-CQD/Fe2O3/GCE and can be applicable to the sensitive detection of OLZ. Fig. 6B displays the linear relationship between the oxidation peak current and concentration of OLZ; the linear regression equation is E pa (V) = 4.0202(±0.1218) [OLZ] + 0.5252(±0.0197) and the correlation coefficient is R2 = 0.9973.

Fig. 7A exhibits various scanning rate effect of electro-oxidation response of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE in 0.1 M PB solution (pH 7). In the presence of 200 μM OLZ, an increment of scanning rate from 20 to 180 mV s−1 leads to the increase in oxidation current. A plot between the oxidation peak current (I pa) and the square root of the scanning rate gives the linear plot (Fig. 7B), which is represented by I pa (μA) = 0.1464 (±0.0037) x − 0.0939(±0.0373); R2 = 0.9955. Further, it directly implies that the oxidation process of OLZ at S-CQD/Fe2O3/GCE is a diffusion-controlled electron transfer process. From the data, it is concluded that the obtained CV curves are because of the detection of OLZ diffusion over the surface of S-CQD/Fe2O3/GCE. The above results help in concluding that the process is a diffusion-controlled electrode reaction [2]. The number of electron transfer at electrode reaction (n) in the potential scanning rate from 20 to 180 mV s1 was calculated using the Eq. (2), where Q is the charge (C mol−1) used by the surface process as calculated by the integration of the surface area under the peak.
(2)
n =
4 IpRT
FQv
From the slope of a peak current vs. square root of the scan rate plot, the transferred number of electrons is calculated to be 2.

The electrochemical determination of OLZ was carried out at the surface of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE by DPV. Compared to CV, DPV is a more sensitive method for the determination of OLZ. The DPV responses of S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE with the increment of different concentration of OLZ range from 0.1 to 35 μM in the PB solution (pH 7) as shown in Fig. 8A. The results revealed that the S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite-modified electrode might offer good electrocatalytic activity towards OLZ sensing. From the slope of the calibration plot (Fig. 8B), the calculated sensitivity is of about 0.88 μA μM−1 cm−2. On comparison with other reports in the literature, the present method has shown the higher sensitivity. In addition to this, linear regression value I pa (V) = 0.0571(±0.0041) [OLZ] + 1.2557(±0.0014), correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9914 were obtained from the calibration plot. In addition to that limit of detection (LOD) also calculated from the calibration plot which is of about 0.006 μM (S/N = 3). The analytical parameters of the presently modified electrode are compared with OLZ sensor of previous reports, which are given in Table 1 . The obtained results suggest that S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE possess good sensitivity and shows very low detection limit.

The interference study of the developed sensor is analyzed by DPV in the occurrence of 100-fold excess concentration of valine (Val), glucose (Glu), sucrose (Suc), fructose (Fru), cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), copper (Cu2+) ion, potassium (K+) ion, magnesium (Mg2+) ion and potassium (K+) ion and their results are shown in Fig. 9 . The common interferents have not meaningfully influenced the peak current intensity. There was no response for Val, Glu, Suc, Fru, and Cys at S-CQD/Fe2O3/GCE; while AA and UA showed a small response. Even though, the oxidation peak potential of OLZ was not disturbed by the AA and UA. Furthermore, the selectivity can be explained through the redox mechanism of OLZ as shown in Scheme 2 . Initially NH group in the diazepine ring (1) of OLZ is oxidized to form the radical cation (2). Still the NH group is polarizable, thus increasing the acidity of NH group [33]. So, the hydrogen bond can readily occur with the carbonyl group of the S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite and the electron transfer readily occurs through hydrogen bond. Thus, the S-CQDs can perform as a promoter for the specific interaction. For this reason, H+ ion is deprotonated from amine group to form the aminyl radical (3), which further losses one electron and forms the oxidized product (4) due to the good electrocatalytic nature of Fe2O3 in the nanocomposite. The obtained results clearly specify that the interference of common interfering molecules was unnecessary for the sensitive detection of OLZ at S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE and also it recommended the decent selectivity of the developed sensor.

Fig. 10A displays the accumulation time for the oxidation of OLZ, analyzed in CV at S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE in the existence of 200 μM OLZ in PB solution (pH 7) with the scanning rate of 50 mV s−1. At different accumulation time from 0 to 240 s, CV was recorded; whereas at 90 s higher oxidation peak current was obtained. Thus, the proposed sensor was optimized with an appropriate accumulation time of 90 s, for the active oxidation of OLZ. In order to verify the reproducibility of S-CQD/Fe2O3/GCE, different five independent electrodes were prepared, then CV was carried out for the detection of OLZ (200 μM) in PB solution (pH 7) at the scanning rate of 50 mV s−1, the results are shown in Fig. 10B and they suggest high reproducibility to detect the OLZ. The operational stability of the S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE was testified by running the CV for 50 continuous cycles, without (Fig. 10C) and with (Fig. 10D) the presence of 200 μM OLZ. The results show only 1.37% loss from the initial value after 50 cycles and signifies the good operational stability of the electrode. Thus, the above data shows good reproducibility, operational stability of the proposed sensor and also it is highly applicable for the real sample analysis.

The practical applicability of the developed sensor was investigated in human-urine samples by standard addition method using DPV. Prior to the experiment, human-urine samples were collected from the volunteers in which a known amount of OLZ was spiked and no further sample preparations were required. The standard addition method was used to examine the changes in the analytical signal with respect to the addition. The optimized DPV procedure was applied to determine the OLZ concentration in spiked urine samples and the obtained calibration peaks are shown in Fig. 11A, B. Table 2 represents the recovery results of OLZ in urine samples, which were calculated from the linear regression equation (Fig. 11C, D). The pretreatment steps like extraction and evaporation are not required for the determination of OLZ in the spiked urine samples. The above results show the reproducibility and good sensitivity of the S-CQDs/Fe2O3/GCE towards the OLZ determination in real samples.

In conclusion, we have developed a simplistic route to synthesize S-CQDs and S-CQDs decorated iron oxide nanocomposite (S-CQDs/Fe2O3) via hydrothermal method. The experimental data specified that the introduction of S-CQDs on α-Fe2O3 NPs improved the conductivity and the surface area. The novel S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite-modified electrode possesses high surface area than other modified electrodes, which renders the excellent electro-oxidation towards the OLZ. The developed sensor showed low detection limit and good sensitivity and selectivity. The achieved electrochemical performance can be attributed to the phase stability of the α-Fe2O3 NPs and the synergetic effect of the S-CQDs in the nanocomposite. Thus, it is ascertained that S-CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite can be extensively useful for practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the UGC – BSR (25-1/2014–2015(BSR)/7–88/2007(BSR), Dt.:15th-Oct-2015) fellowship, India and the Alagappa University Research Fund (AURF) (AU: AURF Start-up Research Grant: 2015-16, Dt.: 10.02.2016) and The Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST 106-2113-M-027-003) for the financial support.
